# Project Memory — test-memory
<!-- Generated by memory-core on 2026-05-13 — auto-sync keeps this fresh; manual refresh: memory-core sync -->

Architecture: Backend: NestJS
Stack: Typescript

## Retrieved Memories
_Pulled from central memory via semantic search on "Backend: NestJS Typescript"_

- [rule · nestjs] Services own all business logic: Every business rule, calculation, and orchestration lives in a service. Services are @Injectable() and framework-agnostic — they never import @nestjs/common HTTP decorators or Express/Fastify types.
  Why: Business logic that imports HTTP types cannot run in a CLI, queue worker, or test without an HTTP context. Framework-agnostic services can be tested with plain unit tests — no HTTP setup, no mocking of request objects.
  Tags: services, nestjs

- [rule · nestjs] EventEmitter for in-process side effects: Use @nestjs/event-emitter to decouple side effects (send email, invalidate cache, log analytics) from the main business operation. Emit events from services, handle them in dedicated listener classes.
  Why: Calling EmailService directly from UserService creates a direct dependency — if the email service throws, the user creation fails. An event emitter decouples them: the primary operation succeeds regardless of what happens in listeners, and listeners can be added without touching the service.
  Tags: events, async, nestjs

- [rule · nestjs] ConfigModule with schema validation at startup: Use ConfigModule.forRoot() with a Joi or zod validation schema. The app must fail to start if required env vars are missing or have the wrong type. Never access process.env directly anywhere in the app.
  Why: An app that starts without a required env var crashes at runtime when the var is first accessed — possibly minutes after deployment. Schema validation at startup fails immediately with a clear message. The error happens before any traffic hits the app.
  Tags: config, environment, nestjs

- [pattern · nestjs] Use forRootAsync for config-dependent modules: Dynamic modules (database, cache) use forRoot() for static config and forRootAsync() when config depends on other providers like ConfigService.
  Why: Passing hardcoded config to TypeOrmModule.forRoot() means different values per environment require code changes. forRootAsync() injects ConfigService — the same code runs in every environment, values come from the environment, not the source.
  Tags: modules, config, nestjs

- [rule] API versioning: Version all public APIs from day one via URL prefix (/v1/) or header. Never make breaking changes to existing versions.
  Tags: api, versioning

- [rule] Dependency updates on a schedule: Review and update dependencies at least monthly. Use automated tools (Dependabot, Renovate) to get PRs for new versions. Never ignore security advisories.
  Why: Outdated dependencies are the most common source of vulnerabilities in production systems. Most supply-chain attacks exploit known CVEs in packages that were never updated. Automated PRs make updates low-friction — they can be reviewed and merged in minutes.
  Tags: security, dependencies, devops

- [rule] Never expose internal IDs in public APIs: Use UUIDs or slugs as public identifiers in API URLs and responses. Never expose sequential database integer IDs to clients.
  Why: Sequential IDs leak your table size (user #4 means you have ~4 users), enable enumeration attacks (fetch /users/1, /users/2 ...), and make it trivial to guess other users' resource IDs. UUIDs are opaque and non-enumerable.
  Tags: security, api, privacy

- [rule] Sanitize user-generated content: Sanitize all user-generated content before rendering. Escape HTML, strip scripts, and use CSP headers to prevent XSS.
  Tags: security, xss, sanitization

- [rule · nestjs] Typed config with registerAs namespaces: Group related config using registerAs("database", () => ({ url: process.env.DATABASE_URL })). Inject with @InjectConfig("database") for typed access. Never use magic strings to access config keys.
  Why: this.configService.get("DATABASE_URL") returns unknown — a typo in the key name returns undefined and causes a silent runtime crash. Typed namespaced configs are autocomplete-friendly and catch key name errors at compile time.
  Tags: config, typescript, nestjs

- [rule · nestjs] Throw NestJS exceptions from services: Services throw NotFoundException, ForbiddenException, BadRequestException, ConflictException — not raw Error objects. The global exception filter catches and formats them with the correct HTTP status automatically.
  Why: Throwing raw Error from a service forces the controller or filter to guess the status code. NestJS exceptions carry the status code with them — the filter reads it and sets the correct HTTP status. NotFoundException makes intent obvious.
  Tags: exceptions, services, nestjs

- [rule · nestjs] Migrations for all schema changes — never synchronize: Never use synchronize: true in any environment other than a throw-away local database. All schema changes are made through migrations generated via TypeORM CLI and committed to source control.
  Why: synchronize: true drops and recreates columns to match your entity definition. On a production database this destroys data. Migrations are explicit, reversible, and reviewable — every schema change is tracked in source control alongside the code that requires it.
  Tags: database, migrations, nestjs

- [rule] Soft deletes: Use soft deletes (deleted_at timestamp) for user-generated data. Hard delete only for PII removal on request.
  Tags: database, soft-delete

- [rule] Correlation IDs: Attach a unique correlation/trace ID to every request. Propagate it through all downstream calls. Include it in logs and error responses.
  Tags: observability, tracing, api

- [rule] DTOs for API responses: Use DTOs for all API responses. Never expose raw database models to the client.
  Tags: api, dto, response

- [pattern · nestjs] PartialType for update DTOs: Extend CreateUserDto with PartialType(CreateUserDto) to create UpdateUserDto. All fields become optional automatically with no duplication of validators.
  Why: Duplicating DTO properties in an Update class means every change to the Create DTO must be manually mirrored in the Update DTO. PartialType inherits all properties and validators and makes them optional — the definition lives in one place.
  Tags: dto, nestjs

- [rule · nestjs] Never return entities directly from controllers: Controller methods never return TypeORM/Prisma entities. Always map to a response DTO before returning. Use plainToInstance(ResponseDto, entity) or a dedicated mapper class.
  Why: Returning a TypeORM entity sends all columns to the client — including passwords, internal IDs, and audit fields. It also couples your API shape to your database schema: any column rename becomes a breaking API change.
  Tags: dto, security, entities, nestjs

- [rule] CORS explicitly configured: Configure CORS with an explicit allowlist of origins. Never use wildcard (*) for authenticated APIs.
  Tags: security, cors, api

- [rule · nestjs] Global API prefix and versioning: Set a global API prefix (api) and version prefix (v1) in main.ts via app.setGlobalPrefix(). Do not mix versioning strategies across controllers.
  Why: Inconsistent versioning — some routes with v1, some without — forces clients to maintain a map of which endpoints are versioned. A global prefix applied in main.ts means every route is versioned uniformly with one configuration change.
  Tags: versioning, controllers, nestjs

- [rule · nestjs] CoreModule for app-wide singletons: Create a CoreModule (imported once in AppModule) for global singletons: database connection, config, logger, event emitter. Never import infrastructure providers in feature modules directly.
  Why: Importing infrastructure into every feature module creates hidden coupling between features and infrastructure. CoreModule is the single place that wires infrastructure — feature modules consume it through DI without knowing where it came from.
  Tags: modules, core-module, nestjs
